scope: PliegoRS G2 data, actions, sessions, uploads, outbound HTTP, and runtime cache beta
disclaimer: This is a framework ownership map, not an application compliance claim.

controls.scopeNote: The framework guard allowlists scheme, host, port, segmented path prefixes, redirects, DNS results, and public IP ranges; applications must use the permit with a pinned-address HTTP adapter.
The default application session cookie is Secure and uses the __Host- prefix; the policy rejects disabling Secure for that prefix.
The default cookie is SameSite=Lax and SameSite=None requires Secure; applications own the documented reason and CSRF treatment for cross-site workflows.
The application-session default is host-only and named __Host-pliego-session; the contract has no Domain attribute surface.
The default session cookie is HttpOnly and the opaque token is emitted only through Set-Cookie; claims remain in the server-side store.
Unsafe action admission validates configured Origin policy plus a session-bound, action-bound, revision-bound CSRF token; applications still declare which actions are browser-originated.
The multipart policy bounds encoded, decoded, per-part, file, field, count, name, and temporary-storage sizes; applications own permitted content types and malicious-file handling.
Temporary upload paths use framework-generated random names inside a canonical operator directory; the user-supplied filename is advisory and never forms a path.
Session policies require bounded idle and absolute lifetimes; applications and operators own the risk-based duration and re-authentication rationale.
Opaque session tokens are verified against the configured backend SessionStore before claims are returned; cookie contents alone never authenticate a request.
Every session create and rotation operation generates a new dynamic opaque reference token rather than accepting a static application secret.
Session reference tokens contain 32 bytes generated by the operating-system CSPRNG and are encoded as 43 URL-safe characters without padding.
The rotation primitive atomically replaces the prior server-side token, and the reference login rotates at the authentication boundary across replicas.
Stored sessions carry an absolute expiry that idle refresh cannot extend; expired records are revoked before claims are returned.
Revocation removes the backend reference record, and the two-replica corpus proves the revoked token cannot be reused through another runtime instance.
PliegoRS seals required resource capabilities and authorization hooks, while each application remains responsible for documenting its function and data authorization rules.
The framework requires an explicit authorization result before action execution and capability checks at resource acquisition; object-level policy remains application logic.
Authorization executes in the trusted server action path shared by plain HTML and enhanced clients; JavaScript state cannot grant resource authority.
Cache domains and typed partitions fail closed when private identity or tenant input is absent, preventing sensitive entries from entering the public runtime cache.
The reference login, dashboard, form errors, and redirects emit Cache-Control no-store; applications remain responsible for applying equivalent browser policy to every sensitive response.
Route parameters, query values, headers, cookies, and action bodies remain separate typed sources; strict reference inputs reject unknown action fields before mutation.
Runtime, data, action, cache, invalidation, upload, HTTP, session, CSRF, idempotency, and secret diagnostics exclude raw user data and secret material.
